1: The tradition of the elders (paradosis ton presbyteron) refers to the oral Torah — the Pharisaic tradition of applying written Torah to daily life through accumulated rabbinic interpretation. This tradition was later codified in the Mishnah (ca. 200 CE) and Talmud. The specific dispute about handwashing (v.2) was not a Decalogue commandment but a purity extension developed from the Levitical washings required of priests; the Pharisees extended this priestly requirement to all Jews as an expression of the priestly-people theology. Jesus disputes not Torah itself but the tradition's authority to override Torah (citing corban as the example where tradition nullified the fifth commandment).
2: The Pharisees’ complaint about the disciples not washing hands before eating refers to the oral Torah’s extension of priestly purity requirements to all Israelites. Exodus 30:17-21 required priests to wash hands and feet before approaching the altar; the Pharisaic tradition extended this to laypeople before meals as an expression of Israel’s "kingdom of priests" calling (Exod 19:6). This was not a hygiene practice but a ritual purity action, and it was oral tradition rather than written Torah — making Jesus’ challenge in vv. 3-9 theologically precise.
3: "And why do you break the commandment of God for the sake of your tradition?" — the counter-charge employs the same verb (parabainō = transgress, break) the Pharisees used implicitly about the disciples. The inversion is rhetorically powerful: those who accuse others of transgression are themselves transgressing. The "commandment of God" (entolēn tou theou) is the written Torah, specifically the fifth commandment (Exod 20:12; Deut 5:16); the "tradition of men" is the oral halakha that has overridden it.
4: The fifth commandment — "Honor your father and your mother" (Exod 20:12) and "whoever reviles father or mother must surely die" (Exod 21:17) — was explicit written Torah with capital sanction. The combination of the positive command (honor = support financially) and the negative sanction (death for cursing parents) established the highest obligation of filial piety in Jewish law. Jesus cites both to establish the legal weight of the commandment before showing how tradition has nullified it.
5: The corban vow (Dōron ho ean ex emou ōphelēthēs = "Whatever you might have received from me is a gift [dedicated to God]") was a real halakhic device recorded in the Mishnah (m. Nedarim 9:1) by which a person could declare property as dedicated to the Temple, rendering it unavailable for other uses. The problematic application Jesus cites is using the corban formula specifically to avoid financial support of aging parents: by declaring your support-money as corban, you technically dedicated it to God and could not give it to your parents, yet you could retain use of it yourself. This allowed the letter of piety toward God to nullify the commandment of piety toward parents.
6: "You have made void the word of God for the sake of your tradition" (ēkyrōsate ton logon tou theou dia tēn paradosin hymōn) — the strong verb akuroō (to invalidate, deprive of authority) was a legal term for nullifying a contract or ruling. The charge is not that the tradition was wrong in itself but that the specific application of the corban rule nullified an explicit Torah commandment — a violation of the hermeneutical principle that oral tradition must serve written Torah, not override it.
7: The citation of Isaiah 29:13 — "This people honors me with their lips, but their heart is far from me; in vain do they worship me, teaching as doctrines the commandments of men" — places the Pharisaic tradition problem within the prophetic critique of empty formalism. Isaiah 29 addresses Jerusalem's leaders during the Assyrian crisis who performed the outward forms of worship while their inner orientation was distant from God. Jesus applies the same prophetic critique to the specific mechanism by which tradition has displaced Torah.
8: Isaiah's complaint "this people honors me with their lips, but their heart is far from me" was understood in Second Temple Judaism as an ongoing prophetic indictment with ongoing applicability. The citation is from the LXX version, which differs slightly from the MT. The "lips/heart" contrast activates the covenantal internalization language of Deuteronomy: true covenant faithfulness must be inscribed on the heart (Deut 6:6; 30:14), not merely performed on the lips. External compliance without internal alignment is the structural failure Isaiah diagnosed.
9: "Teaching as doctrines the commandments of men" (didaskontes didaskalias entalmata anthrōpōn) — the elevation of human tradition to the status of divine commandment is the specific error. Mishnah tractate Avot (1:1) specifically instructed rabbis to "make a fence around the Torah" — to create additional protective regulations beyond the Torah’s requirements to prevent accidental violation. The fence-building impulse was legitimate; its abuse was when the fence regulations began to be treated as Torah itself rather than as protective applications of Torah.
10: "Hear and understand" (akousate kai syniete) calls the crowd back into the teaching circle after the private confrontation with the Pharisees. The move from insider conflict to public teaching is deliberate: the purity teaching that follows is addressed to everyone, not just the religious professionals. Jesus publicly challenges a defining feature of Pharisaic practice.
11: "It is not what goes into the mouth that defiles a person, but what comes out of the mouth; this defiles a person" — this principle challenged one of the foundational structures of Second Temple Jewish identity: the Levitical food purity system (Lev 11; Deut 14). The distinction between clean and unclean foods was a major marker of Jewish distinctiveness in the Greco-Roman world; observance of kashrut was one of the most visible signs of Jewish covenant identity. Mark 7:19 adds the editorial note "thus he declared all foods clean" — an implication the early church had to work through (Acts 10:9-16; Gal 2:11-14).
12: "Do you know that the Pharisees were offended (eskandalisthēsan) when they heard this saying?" — the disciples’ question about the Pharisees’ offense reveals their concern about institutional relationships. The disciples were themselves Jews operating within the synagogue system and were aware of the social consequences of alienating Pharisaic leadership. The verb skandalizō (to offend, to cause to stumble) was used for the kind of offense that led to severance of relationship — the disciples recognized that Jesus’ teaching had created a serious social rupture.
13: "Every plant that my heavenly Father has not planted will be rooted up" — the agricultural image of planting and uprooting was applied in Jewish literature to individuals and communities established by God versus those that were human constructions. The metaphor draws on Isaiah’s vineyard imagery (Isa 5:1-7) and Jeremiah’s planting imagery (Jer 1:10; 24:6). Jesus’ dismissal of the Pharisees’ offense as irrelevant — "let them alone; they are blind guides" — is a judgment that places the Pharisaic leadership outside the scope of God’s eschatological planting.
14: "They are blind guides of the blind. And if the blind lead the blind, both will fall into a pit" — the blind-leader proverb draws on a social reality (blind persons navigating unfamiliar terrain) that was universally understood. In Jewish wisdom literature, the blind person was a figure for moral/spiritual ignorance rather than physical disability (Isa 29:9-10; 42:18-19; Zeph 1:17). The leader who cannot see cannot lead; those who follow sightless leaders share their fate. The Pharisees’ failure is not just personal but fatal for those they lead.
15: Peter’s request for explanation (phrase = interpret for us) treats the parable of inner defilement (v. 11) as a mashal requiring authoritative unpacking. The disciples’ incomprehension after the Beelzebul controversy (12:10) and the parable discourse (13:36) creates a pattern of misunderstanding that characterizes their learning: they hear but need private explanation. The request "explain the parable to us" mirrors the request at 13:36 ("explain to us the parable of the weeds").
16: "Are you also still without understanding?" — Jesus’ expression of surprise at the disciples’ incomprehension after all his teaching echoes the OT tradition of divine frustration with Israel’s dullness (Isa 6:9-10; Jer 5:21; Ezek 12:2). The disciples’ persistent incomprehension is not presented as stupidity but as a spiritual condition that the resurrection and Pentecost will ultimately transform. Their slowness of understanding serves the narrative purpose of requiring Jesus to explain clearly what his teaching means.
17: The physiology of digestion (panta ta eisporeuomena eis to stoma eis tēn koilian chōrei kai eis aphedrōna ekballetai) — the stomach receives food and the latrine evacuates it — grounds the purity teaching in observable bodily reality. Everything that enters the mouth passes through the body and exits; nothing ingested permanently contaminates the person’s identity. This is a physiological argument against the ritual-purity logic of food prohibitions: the body itself does not retain the defilement of what it eats.
18: "But what comes out of the mouth proceeds from the heart, and this defiles a person" — the heart (kardia) in Hebrew/Jewish anthropology is the center of will, intention, and thought — not merely emotion. Prov 4:23 ("keep your heart with all vigilance, for from it flow the springs of life") is the Wisdom background; Jer 17:9 ("the heart is deceitful above all things") and Ezek 36:26 ("a new heart I will give you") establish the heart as the site of either covenant faithfulness or corruption. Defilement originates in the will, not in dietary contact.
19: The list of evils proceeding from the heart — evil thoughts, murder, adultery, sexual immorality, theft, false witness, slander (ponēroi dialogismoi, phonoi, moicheiai, porneiai, klopai, pseudomartyiai, blasphēmiai) — parallels the Decalogue sequence (sixth, seventh, eighth, ninth commandments) with an opening category of evil intentions. This suggests that the list is structured around the second table of the Decalogue (commandments governing human relationships), with "evil thoughts" as the intentional precondition for the specific violations. Sin begins in the will before it is enacted.
20: "These are what defile a person. But to eat with unwashed hands does not defile a person" — the conclusion directly answers the opening controversy (v. 2). The entire purity teaching — from the corban critique through the inner-defilement principle to the list of heart-evils — has been a single sustained argument that the Pharisaic handwashing tradition is both secondary (human tradition) and mislocated (targeting the wrong source of defilement). The conclusion restores the original charge and answers it definitively.
21: Jesus’ withdrawal to the district of Tyre and Sidon (anechōrēsen eis ta merē Tyrou kai Sidōnos) moves the ministry into Phoenician coastal territory — modern Lebanon. This is the only time in Matthew’s Gospel that Jesus leaves Israelite territory. Tyre and Sidon were prosperous Phoenician port cities with large mixed populations; the Syro-Phoenician (or Canaanite, as Matthew describes the woman) population was ethnically distinct from the Jewish population. Jesus’ presence in this territory is presented as withdrawal rather than mission to Gentiles — he came to be away from the controversy in Galilee.
22: The woman is identified as a "Canaanite" (Chananaia) — Matthew’s archaic term activates the OT category of the paradigmatic enemy and other, the people Israel displaced in the conquest. Mark 7:26 uses the more contemporary "Syro-Phoenician." Matthew’s "Canaanite" framing intensifies the ethnic-boundary transgression: this is not a neutral Gentile but a representative of Israel’s ancient rivals. Her address — "Lord, Son of David, have mercy on me" — uses the Israelite messianic title, crossing the ethnic boundary in her terminology even as she crosses it physically by approaching Jesus.
23: "He did not answer her a word" — Jesus’ silence is a social statement in a culture where acknowledged persons received acknowledgment. The disciples’ request — "send her away, for she is crying out after us" — was either a request to grant her petition (so she will stop following) or to dismiss her (so she will go away). The ambiguity in the Greek allows both readings; the disciples’ concern is pragmatic management of a socially awkward situation rather than a theological position on Gentile mission.
24: "I was sent only to the lost sheep of the house of Israel" — the limitation of Jesus’ primary mission to Israel is consistent with the Mission Discourse (10:5-6: "Do not go among the Gentiles... go rather to the lost sheep of Israel"). In Matthew’s theology, the Gentile mission is the post-resurrection commission (28:19: "make disciples of all nations"); the pre-resurrection mission is the covenant people’s restoration. Jesus’ statement to the woman reflects this staged mission theology, making her persistence and faith all the more remarkable.
25: The woman’s prostration (prosekynei autō̄) and simple petition "Lord, help me" (Kyrie, boēthei moi) is the most elemental form of supplication. In the ancient world, prostration before a powerful figure was the posture of absolute petition — one gave up any claim to standing or honor and threw oneself entirely on the other’s mercy. The combination of prostration and minimal verbal request represents the complete absence of self-assertion.
26: "It is not right to take the children’s bread and throw it to the dogs" — the "dogs" (kynarion = small dogs, household dogs) was a standard Jewish term of disparagement for Gentiles, reflecting the purity framework that associated Gentiles with uncleanliness. The diminutive form (kynarion) softens the insult slightly (household pet rather than street scavenger) but the ethnic boundary language remains sharp. Jesus’ use of the metaphor is pedagogical: he is testing whether her faith can navigate the boundary rather than asserting a personal conviction about Gentile worthlessness.
27: The woman’s response — "Yes, Lord, yet even the dogs eat the crumbs that fall from their masters’ table" — is a brilliant halakhic argument within the metaphor’s framework. She does not deny the priority of the children (Israel) or claim equality with them; she accepts the metaphor and argues from within it that the dogs’ inclusion does not require the children’s exclusion. The household table produces enough for both. The argument is theologically precise: Gentile blessing is not the depletion of Jewish blessing but the overflow of it — a principle Paul will articulate in Romans 11.
28: "O woman, great is your faith!" — the exclamation marks this as a unique moment in Matthew: the only person praised for "great faith" by name (the centurion in 8:10 received the comparison "not even in Israel have I found such faith"). Her persistence, her theological creativity within the metaphor, and her willingness to accept a subsidiary position while still pressing her claim constitute the model of faith under apparent obstacle. The healing of her daughter at that moment ("from that moment her daughter was healed") is immediate and distance-based, paralleling the centurion’s servant healing.
29: Jesus’ return to the Sea of Galilee and ascent of a mountain (anabas eis to oros ekathēto ekei) near the Decapolis (implied by Mark 7:31’s parallel: through the Decapolis) places this healing ministry in Gentile territory. The Decapolis was a league of ten (originally) Hellenistic cities east and southeast of the Sea of Galilee (Scythopolis, Gadara, Hippos, Pella, Philadelphia, and others) with predominantly Gentile populations and Greek cultural institutions. The mountain setting and the seated teaching posture echo the Sermon on the Mount geography but in Gentile territory.
30: The crowd bringing "the lame, the blind, the crippled, the mute, and many others" (chōlous, typhlous, kullous, kōphous kai pollous heterous) in Gentile territory creates a deliberate echo of the Galilean healing summaries (4:23-24; 9:35). The categories of healing match the messianic catalogue Jesus cited to John (11:5: blind see, lame walk, deaf hear) — now occurring among Gentiles. The crowd’s glorification "of the God of Israel" (edoxasan ton theon Israēl) is unique: Gentile crowds praising Israel’s God is an eschatological anticipation of the nations’ inclusion (cf. Isa 2:2-4; 45:23; Zeph 3:9).
31: The phrase "they glorified the God of Israel" (ton theon Israēl) is significant precisely because Matthew uses it in a Gentile setting. The God of Israel is worshiped by non-Israelites on the basis of what they have witnessed — a fulfillment of the Isaianic vision of the nations recognizing YHWH through Israel’s restoration (Isa 52:10; 66:18-21). The specific national designation "of Israel" underlines that the Gentiles are not worshiping a generic deity but the covenant God of a specific people.
32: Jesus’ compassion for the crowd in the wilderness after three days parallels the five-thousand feeding (14:14) but in a distinctly Gentile context. Three days in the wilderness without food would have been a significant physical stress; the detail grounds the miracle in an urgency that was not a test but a genuine welfare crisis. The disciples’ question "where are we to get enough bread in such a desolate place (erēmia) to feed so great a crowd?" repeats the resource-inadequacy that preceded the five-thousand feeding, setting up the parallel miracle.
33: The disciples’ question — "Where are we to get enough bread in such a desolate place to feed so great a crowd?" — is often read as forgetful incomprehension after the five-thousand feeding. But the geography is different: they are in Gentile Decapolis territory, not in Bethsaida-adjacent Jewish territory. The disciples’ question may reflect genuine uncertainty about whether the pattern of the five-thousand feeding would apply in Gentile territory — whether the kingdom’s abundance would extend to non-Israelites.
34: Seven loaves and a few small fish (ichthydia oliga) — the smaller starting inventory compared to the five loaves and two fish of the five-thousand feeding corresponds to the larger crowd and equally miraculous outcome. Seven as a number had cultural resonance in both Jewish and Gentile contexts; in rabbinic tradition, seven represented completeness. The seven baskets collected after the four-thousand feeding (spyridas = large rope baskets, distinct from the Jewish kophinoi of the five-thousand) may reflect Gentile cultural context through the different basket type.
35: The command to sit on the ground (anapesein epi tēn gēn) and the blessing, breaking, and distribution follow the same eucharistic pattern as the five-thousand feeding (14:19). The repeated structure links the two feedings as parallel events: the same pattern of Jesus’ provision for those who follow him operates in both Jewish (Bethsaida) and Gentile (Decapolis) settings. The early church reading these narratives would have recognized the sacramental form and the missional implication.
36: The giving of thanks (eucharistēsas) before breaking and distributing is the standard Jewish meal-blessing form (birkat hamazon) — now performed in Gentile territory for a Gentile crowd. This detail reinforces that the kingdom’s provision is not ethnically restricted: the same Jesus who blesses bread for five thousand Jews blesses bread for four thousand Gentiles using the same liturgical form.
37: Seven baskets of fragments gathered (heptà spyridas plēreis tōn perisseumatōn) — the seven baskets match the seven loaves, suggesting completeness of provision. The different basket word (spyris = large wicker basket, sometimes used for rope-made containers) from the five-thousand’s kophinos (the distinctively Jewish wicker basket) appears in Acts 9:25 (the basket used to lower Paul from Damascus — a Gentile city). The basket distinction may be another marker of the Gentile context.
38: Four thousand men besides women and children — the crowd count is given the same formula as the five-thousand ("besides women and children"), using the same conventional census method of counting adult males. The four-thousand count in Gentile territory, smaller than the five-thousand in Jewish territory, may reflect the proportional distribution of population in each region or may simply be realistic narrative variation. The parallel structure of the two feedings (crowd + disciples + wilderness + inadequate food + miracle + surplus + count) is too deliberate to be accidental.
39: Jesus’ dismissal of the crowd and crossing to Magadan (or Magdala in some manuscripts) brings the ministry back to the Jewish western shore of the Sea of Galilee. Magdala (modern Migdal) was a prosperous fishing town and fish-processing center on the western shore, known in rabbinic literature as Tarichaeae. The return from Gentile Decapolis territory to Jewish Galilean territory completes the literary movement of the Tyre/Sidon and Decapolis section: Jesus briefly extended his ministry into Gentile regions before returning to the central arena of Galilean controversy.
